name: Archaea
characteristics: unique membrane lipids with ether linkages
description: One of the three domains of life, comprising single-celled prokaryotes distinct from bacteria. Archaea have unique biochemical features and are often found in extreme environments, though they are also common in ocean and soil ecosystems.

name: Bacteria
description: The most abundant and diverse domain of life, comprising microscopic single-celled organisms without a membrane-bound nucleus. Bacteria inhabit nearly every environment on Earth and play critical roles in nutrient cycling, decomposition, and human health.

name: Eukaryota
description: The domain of life encompassing organisms whose cells have a membrane-bound nucleus and complex organelles. Includes all animals, plants, fungi, and protists. Eukaryotic cells are generally larger and more structurally complex than prokaryotic cells.

name: Animalia
description: Kingdom of multicellular eukaryotes that obtain nutrition by consuming other organisms. The most species-rich kingdom, animals occupy virtually every habitat on Earth.

name: Archaebacteria
description: In the six-kingdom system, Archaebacteria encompasses the ancient prokaryotes with biochemistry distinct from true bacteria. Corresponding to the domain Archaea, they thrive in extreme conditions such as hot springs, salt lakes, and anaerobic environments.

name: Eubacteria
description: The true bacteria kingdom, representing the largest and most metabolically diverse group of organisms. Eubacteria include pathogens, decomposers, nitrogen-fixers, and photosynthesizers, and are indispensable to all ecosystems.

name: Fungi
description: Kingdom of eukaryotes that absorb nutrients by secreting enzymes to break down organic matter externally. Fungi are essential decomposers in ecosystems and include economically important species used in food production, medicine, and biotechnology.

name: Plantae
description: Kingdom of multicellular photosynthetic eukaryotes that form the base of most terrestrial food webs. Plants colonized land approximately 470 million years ago and now dominate terrestrial ecosystems worldwide.

name: Protista
description: A diverse and paraphyletic kingdom encompassing eukaryotes that do not fit neatly into the animal, plant, or fungal kingdoms. Includes algae, protozoa, and slime molds. Many are ecologically important primary producers or pathogens.

name: Annelida
description: The segmented worms, a diverse phylum of invertebrates found in marine, freshwater, and terrestrial environments. Annelids play crucial roles in soil aeration and nutrient cycling.

name: Arthropoda
description: The largest animal phylum by species count, comprising over 80% of all described animal species. Arthropods are found in virtually every habitat on Earth and include ecologically and economically critical species.

name: Ascomycota
description: The largest fungal phylum, distinguished by reproductive structures called asci that contain ascospores. Includes yeasts critical to baking and brewing, molds that produce antibiotics, and edible truffles.

name: Basidiomycota
description: The club fungi phylum, producing spores on basidiocarps (mushrooms, brackets, puffballs). Includes the majority of familiar edible mushrooms as well as many plant pathogens such as rusts and smuts.

name: Chordata
description: The phylum containing all vertebrates plus a few invertebrate groups such as tunicates and lancelets. Chordates are defined by four key features present at some point in their life cycle, most prominently the notochord.

name: Cnidaria
characteristics: two body forms: polyp and medusa
description: Marine and freshwater invertebrates characterized by stinging cells called cnidocytes. Corals within this phylum build reef ecosystems that support approximately 25% of all marine species.

name: Echinodermata
description: A phylum of exclusively marine invertebrates with a unique water vascular system used for movement, feeding, and gas exchange. Echinoderms are important members of benthic marine ecosystems.

name: Mollusca
description: The second largest animal phylum, encompassing a highly diverse range of body plans unified by the presence of a mantle and, in most species, a shell. Includes ecologically, commercially, and culturally important species.

name: Nematoda
description: Roundworms are among the most abundant animals on Earth; a single handful of soil can contain thousands of individuals. Nematodes occupy roles as decomposers, predators, and parasites in virtually every habitat.

name: Platyhelminthes
description: Flatworms are the simplest bilaterally symmetrical animals, with no body cavity and a single opening serving as both mouth and anus (except in some). Many are parasites causing significant human diseases such as schistosomiasis.

name: Porifera
description: Sponges are the simplest and most ancient multicellular animals, lacking true tissues and organs. They filter vast quantities of water through their porous bodies, playing important roles in nutrient cycling in marine environments.

name: Angiosperms
description: Flowering plants are the most diverse and widespread division of the plant kingdom, comprising about 90% of all plant species. They dominate most terrestrial ecosystems and provide the vast majority of human food crops.

name: Bryophyta (Mosses)
characteristics: no true roots, stems, or leaves
description: Mosses are non-vascular land plants that form dense green mats in moist habitats. They were among the first plants to colonize land. Sphagnum peat mosses are ecologically important in carbon sequestration and bog ecosystems.

name: Gymnosperms
description: Seed plants whose seeds are not enclosed within a fruit. Gymnosperms dominated terrestrial ecosystems before the rise of flowering plants and remain important components of boreal, montane, and temperate forests.

name: Pteridophyta (Ferns)
description: Ferns and fern allies are vascular plants that reproduce via spores rather than seeds. They were dominant land plants during the Carboniferous period. Today they are important components of forest understories worldwide.

name: Actinopterygii
description: Ray-finned fishes constitute the largest class of vertebrates, accounting for roughly half of all living vertebrate species. They are found in virtually every aquatic habitat, from deep ocean to high-altitude mountain streams.

name: Amphibia
description: Amphibians were the first vertebrates to colonize land, retaining ties to aquatic environments for reproduction. They are highly sensitive environmental indicators; global populations are declining due to habitat loss, disease, and climate change.

name: Aves
description: Birds are feathered, winged dinosaurs that evolved approximately 150 million years ago. They occupy every habitat on Earth including Antarctica. Approximately 10,000 living species are recognized, ranging from tiny hummingbirds to the flightless ostrich.

name: Insecta
description: Insects are the most species-rich class of animals on Earth, with over a million described species and potentially several million undescribed. They play fundamental roles as pollinators, decomposers, and prey in virtually every terrestrial ecosystem.

name: Mammalia
description: Mammals are endothermic vertebrates that nurse their young with milk produced by mammary glands. They have diversified to occupy marine, aerial, and terrestrial niches, ranging in size from the bumblebee bat to the blue whale.

name: Reptilia
description: A paraphyletic class of amniotes adapted for life on land, with dry scaly skin that reduces water loss. Reptiles dominated terrestrial ecosystems during the Mesozoic era. Modern species include lizards, snakes, turtles, and crocodilians.
